symptoms.name: Jaundice
symptoms.description: Yellowing of the skin and whites of the eyes, caused by bilirubin building up faster than the liver can process it. Extremely common in newborns — affects up to 60% of full-term babies.
symptoms.normalRange: Mild yellowing in the first 2–5 days is common, peaks around days 3–5, and usually clears by 2 weeks in full-term babies. Breastfed babies may have visible jaundice slightly longer (up to 3 weeks).
callDoctorIf: Jaundice appears in the first 24 hours of life
Yellow colour spreads to the belly, arms, or legs
Baby is very difficult to wake for feeds
Baby is not feeding well or producing fewer wet nappies
Jaundice persists beyond 3 weeks in a full-term baby
Yellow colour of skin is deepening after day 5
goERIf: Baby is limp, unresponsive, or has a high-pitched cry
Baby develops a high fever with jaundice
You were discharged with instructions to return if jaundice worsened and it has
symptoms.ageNote: Most relevant in the first 2 weeks of life

symptoms.name: Temperature / Fever
symptoms.description: Newborns cannot regulate body temperature well. A fever in a newborn under 3 months is always taken seriously because their immune systems cannot mount a robust response to serious infection.
symptoms.normalRange: Normal temperature: 36.5–37.5°C (97.7–99.5°F) rectally. Axillary (armpit) readings run 0.5–1°C lower.
callDoctorIf: Temperature over 37.5°C (99.5°F) in a baby under 3 months — call immediately, even at night
Temperature below 36°C — hypothermia in a newborn needs assessment
Baby feels unusually cold, pale, or mottled (blotchy skin pattern)
goERIf: Any fever (38°C or higher rectally) in a baby under 8 weeks — this is a medical emergency
Baby under 3 months with fever and any of: rash, lethargy, difficulty breathing, not feeding
You cannot reach your doctor and baby has a fever under 3 months
symptoms.ageNote: Fever in babies under 3 months always requires immediate medical attention

symptoms.name: Umbilical Cord
symptoms.description: The stump of the umbilical cord typically dries out and falls off between 1–3 weeks. It changes colour from yellow-green to brown to black as it dries — this is normal.
symptoms.normalRange: Stump falls off between 7–21 days. Slight redness directly on the stump and a small amount of clear or slightly bloody discharge when it falls off is normal.
callDoctorIf: Redness spreading to the skin around the base of the cord (omphalitis)
Yellow or green pus or foul smell from the cord
The cord bleeds more than a few drops when it falls off
Stump has not fallen off after 6 weeks
goERIf: Red streaks spreading from the navel area
Baby has a fever along with cord redness or discharge — this could be a serious infection
symptoms.ageNote: Keep cord dry and exposed to air — fold nappy below the stump

symptoms.name: Weight Loss & Gain
symptoms.description: Almost all newborns lose weight in the first few days as they pass meconium and adjust to feeding. This is expected and normal — the question is how much and whether they recover.
symptoms.normalRange: Losing up to 7–10% of birth weight in the first 3–5 days is normal. Baby should be back to birth weight by day 10–14. After that, expect a gain of 150–220g (5–8 oz) per week for the first 3 months.
callDoctorIf: Baby loses more than 10% of birth weight
Not back to birth weight by day 14
Less than 6 wet nappies per day after day 4
No dirty nappies for more than 24 hours in the first month
Consistently gaining less than 150g per week after the first 2 weeks
symptoms.ageNote: Weight is checked at the 2-week and 1-month visits — if concerned, ask for a mid-week weight check

symptoms.name: Nappy Output
symptoms.description: Nappy output is one of the most reliable indicators of whether baby is getting enough to eat.
symptoms.normalRange: Day 1: 1+ wet, 1 meconium (black/tarry). Days 2–4: increasing wet nappies, transitional stool (brown/green). Day 5+: at least 6 heavy wet nappies per day, 3+ yellow seedy stools (breastfed) or less frequent but larger stools (formula).
callDoctorIf: Fewer than 6 wet nappies per day after day 5
Urine looks dark yellow or orange (concentrated)
No dirty nappy for 3+ days in the first month (breastfed babies can go longer after 6 weeks)
Bloody or mucousy stool
White, grey, or chalky stools — possible liver issue
goERIf: Bright red blood in the stool (not just a pink tinge)
Baby appears to be in significant pain when passing stool

symptoms.name: Common Newborn Rashes
symptoms.description: Newborn skin is adjusting to life outside the womb. Many rashes appear in the first weeks and are completely normal.
symptoms.normalRange: Milia (tiny white bumps on nose/cheeks — blocked pores), erythema toxicum (red blotchy rash with white/yellow centres, appears 1–3 days), baby acne (red pimples 2–4 weeks), stork bites (flat pink/red marks on neck or eyelids) — all harmless and self-resolving.
callDoctorIf: Rash with fever
Rash that looks like bruising or purple/red dots that don't fade when pressed (petechiae)
Spreading rash with blistering or open sores
Cradle cap becoming red, cracked, or extending beyond the scalp — may be seborrhoeic dermatitis
goERIf: Non-blanching rash (press a glass to it — if it doesn't fade, this is a medical emergency)
Rash with high fever and baby seems very unwell

symptoms.name: Breathing Patterns
symptoms.description: Newborn breathing looks very different from adult breathing — irregular rhythms and pauses are common.
symptoms.normalRange: Normal rate: 30–60 breaths per minute. Periodic breathing (pauses of up to 10 seconds followed by several rapid breaths) is normal in newborns. Snuffling and snorting sounds are common due to narrow nasal passages.
callDoctorIf: Breathing consistently faster than 60 breaths per minute
Nostrils flaring with each breath
Ribs visible with each breath (retractions)
Grunting with every breath (not just occasionally)
Blue-tinged lips or fingertips after the first few hours of life
Any pause in breathing longer than 20 seconds
goERIf: Baby's lips or face turn blue
Baby stops breathing and doesn't start again within a few seconds
Baby is working very hard to breathe — nostrils flaring, ribs showing, grunting constantly

symptoms.name: Eye Discharge
symptoms.description: Sticky or crusty eyes are very common in newborns due to blocked tear ducts.
symptoms.normalRange: Clear or pale yellow discharge that accumulates at the corner of the eye and makes the eye crusty after sleep — this is usually a blocked tear duct (dacryostenosis) and is harmless. Typically resolves by 12 months.
callDoctorIf: Yellow or green discharge that is thick and copious
Eye is red, swollen, or the eyelid is puffy
Both eyes affected and discharge is increasing
Baby seems sensitive to light or keeps eye closed
goERIf: Significant eye swelling and discharge in the first day or two of life — this could be a birth-acquired infection
symptoms.ageNote: To clean: use a clean cotton ball dampened with sterile saline, wipe from inner corner outward

symptoms.name: Spitting Up vs Vomiting
symptoms.description: Most newborns spit up regularly — it's rarely cause for concern. Vomiting is different in force and volume.
symptoms.normalRange: Spitting up small amounts (tablespoon or less) during or after feeds is normal for the first 6–12 months. The lower oesophageal sphincter is immature — 'a happy spitter' is a real thing.
callDoctorIf: Spitting up blood or material that looks like coffee grounds
Spit-up has yellow or green bile in it
Baby seems to be in pain before, during, or after spitting up (could be reflux)
Baby is not gaining weight adequately due to spit-up volume
Spit-up is increasing in frequency or volume over time
goERIf: Projectile vomiting — vomit shoots out with force across the room, repeatedly — especially from 2–6 weeks (pyloric stenosis)
Any vomiting with blood, bile (yellow/green), or in a very unwell-looking baby

symptoms.name: Excessive Crying / Colic
symptoms.description: Colic is defined as crying for 3+ hours per day, 3+ days per week, for 3+ weeks in an otherwise healthy baby. It typically peaks at 6 weeks and resolves by 3–4 months.
symptoms.normalRange: Some degree of fussiness is universal. A 'fussy period' in the evenings (5–9pm), sometimes called the 'witching hour', is developmentally normal in the first 3 months.
callDoctorIf: Crying is inconsolable and nothing you try makes any difference for extended periods
Baby seems to be in genuine pain (not just fussy) — consider reflux, cow's milk protein intolerance, or other causes
Baby's cry has changed in quality — unusually high-pitched or weak
Crying is accompanied by fever, vomiting, or changes in stool
goERIf: You have reached a point where you are afraid you might hurt the baby — put baby safely in the crib and call for help
Baby cries for hours without any pause and cannot be comforted at all
symptoms.ageNote: If you are struggling with a colicky baby, please seek support. This is genuinely very hard.

symptoms.name: Cradle Cap
symptoms.description: Thick, crusty, yellowish or brownish scaling on the scalp — common from 2 weeks to 6 months.
symptoms.normalRange: Completely harmless seborrhoeic dermatitis of the scalp. Does not itch or bother baby. Usually clears on its own within a few months.
callDoctorIf: Spreading beyond the scalp to the face, ears, or nappy area
Area becomes red, cracked, or appears infected
Baby is scratching excessively
symptoms.ageNote: Management: massage a small amount of olive oil or coconut oil into scalp, leave for 20–30 minutes, gently brush with a soft baby brush, then wash with baby shampoo. Don't pick at scales.

symptoms.name: Nasal Congestion & Sneezing
symptoms.description: Newborns are obligate nose-breathers — any congestion can interfere with feeding and sleep. They also sneeze frequently to clear their nasal passages.
symptoms.normalRange: Frequent sneezing is normal and healthy — a way of clearing the nose. Mild snuffling sounds during sleep or feeding are also normal. A visible nasal crust is common.
callDoctorIf: Thick yellow or green nasal discharge (beyond the first 2 days of life)
Congestion is affecting feeding — baby can't maintain a latch because they can't breathe
Baby has a fever with the congestion
Congestion is causing consistent sleep disruption beyond normal newborn waking
goERIf: Any sign of breathing difficulty with congestion — fast breathing, retractions, or blue colour
symptoms.ageNote: Saline drops before feeds can help clear nasal passages. A bulb syringe can be used if needed but use sparingly.
